preFix.title: Guinea fowl on a smallholding
preFix.subtitle: What they offer, what they demand, and whether they suit your holding.
preFix.excerpt: Guinea fowl are not chickens wearing different clothes. They are semi-wild, extraordinarily vocal, prone to wandering into neighbouring fields, and excellent at tick and insect control. Before acquiring them, it helps to understand the real management picture: housing, behaviour, egg-laying habits, and why they are best kept in groups.
preFix.sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Gamebirds kept for Sporting Purposes (applicable to guinea fowl husbandry context). University of Wisconsin Extension, Raising Guinea Fowl bulletin. Cornell Small Farms Program, Guinea Fowl factsheet.

content.text: Guinea fowl are kept on smallholdings primarily for tick control, where a ranging flock can clear a field of ticks more effectively than any other domestic bird. The trade-off is noise, wandering, and a management style quite different from chickens.
are classed as poultry in the UK and are subject to the same registration, avian influenza, and welfare rules.

Tick control is their strongest practical contribution. A flock of six to ten guinea fowl ranging on a 0.5-acre field will reduce the tick population significantly within one season. They are also highly alert to aerial and ground predators and will set up an alarm call that alerts every other animal on the holding to a hawk, fox, or unfamiliar person. The alarm call is loud enough to be heard clearly across 2-3 acres.

Guinea fowl are difficult to confine. A standard chicken run with a 1.8 m fence will not contain a flock that decides to range beyond it. They need either a very large, well-roofed enclosure or full free-range management with tolerance for them visiting neighbouring fields. They must be trained from the keet stage to come home to roost; birds acquired as adults rarely return to an unfamiliar housing.

Housing requirements are simple: a dry, draught-free building with perches high enough for the birds to feel safe. They prefer to roost 2 m or more off the ground. Unlike chickens, they do not naturally use nesting boxes and prefer to lay in hidden scrapes in long grass.

gives the best chance of a flock that associates your holding with home and returns to roost reliably. Keets need brooder heat for the first six to eight weeks and are less robust than chicken chicks; wet, draughty conditions in the first weeks kill them quickly. Keep them in a warm, dry brooder until feathered, then transition to housing before allowing outdoor access.

The alarm call of a guinea fowl is loud, distinctive, and triggered freely by anything the birds find unusual. This includes passing cars, birds of prey, cats, dogs, and, with some individuals, clouds moving across the sun. If your neighbours are closer than 100 m, it is worth having a conversation before acquiring guinea fowl. The birds call most actively at dawn and when disturbed.

Guinea fowl kept in groups of six or more are significantly calmer than pairs or small groups. A single guinea fowl is miserable. The minimum viable group is four; six to ten is the most practical range for a smallholding tick-control flock.
